paper_title: Reinforcement Learning-Guided Retrieval with Soft Fusion for Robust Multimodal Imitation Learning under Missing Modalities

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Robotic systems perceive the world through multiple input modalities -- including visual camera streams and natural language instructions -- and must select appropriate actions based on these signals. However, assuming the permanent availability of all input devices is unrealistic, as sensors may fail, become occluded, or drop out entirely during deployment. Robust handling of such missing-modality scenarios is therefore essential for real-world robot operation. This paper introduces RL4IL, a reinforcement learning guided method for imitation learning that selects the most suitable action for a given observation by identifying the most relevant expert demonstrations from a training library. A reinforcement learning policy, trained via Proximal Policy Optimisation over Breadth-First Search candidate sets, ranks candidate demonstrations and a soft cross-attention fusion head aggregates their action signals to produce the final prediction. When a modality is missing at inference time, a dedicated per-modality RL retrieval policy identifies donor demonstrations from the training library, and a soft imputation head reconstructs the missing embedding via cross-attention over the top-ranked donors -- without requiring any retraining of the system. Experiments on three LIBERO benchmark suites demonstrate that RL4IL substantially outperforms state-of-the-art imitation learning methods under sensor dropout conditions, while requiring no policy network training. The code can be found at https://github.com/h-ismkhan/Reinforcement-Learning-via-kNN-for-Robotic-Learning-with-Missing-Camera

sections.title: In the name of God
sections.content: REINFORCEMENT LEARNING-GUIDED RETRIEVAL WITH SOFT FUSION FOR ROBUST MULTIMODAL IMITATION LEARNING UNDER MISSING MODALITIES Hassan Ismkhan* and Hamid Bouchahcia Bournemouth University Bournemouth, UK {hismkhan*, abouchachia}@bournemouth.ac.uk *Responsible author

sections.title: ABSTRACT
sections.content: Robotic systems perceive the world through multiple input modalities including visual camera streams and natural language instructions and must select appropriate actions based on these signals. However, assuming the permanent availability of all input devices is unrealistic, as sensors may fail, become occluded, or drop out entirely during deployment. Robust handling of such missing-modality scenarios is therefore essential for real-world robot operation. This paper introduces RL4IL, reinforcement learning guided method for imitation learning that selects the most suitable action for given observation by identifying the most relevant expert demonstrations from training library. reinforcement learning policy, trained via Proximal Policy Optimisation over Breadth-First Search candidate sets, ranks candidate demonstrations and soft cross-attention fusion head aggregates their action signals to produce the final prediction. When modality is missing at inference time, dedicated per-modality RL retrieval policy identifies donor demonstrations from the training library, and soft imputation head reconstructs the missing embedding via cross-attention over the top-ranked donors without requiring any retraining of the system. Experiments on three LIBERO benchmark suites demonstrate that RL4IL substantially outperforms state-of-the-art imitation learning methods under sensor dropout conditions, while requiring no policy network training. The code can be found at https://github.com/h-ismkhan/ Reinforcement-Learning-via-kNN-for-Robotic-Learning-with-Missing-Camera Keywords Missing Modality, Camera Dropout, Robots, Learning

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Robotic manipulation systems deployed in real-world environments must process heterogeneous sensory streams including visual observations from multiple camera viewpoints and natural-language task instructions [1, 2]. While recent advances in imitation learning have demonstrated impressive performance on structured benchmarks [3, 4, 5, 6], these methods operate under tacit assumption that all sensor modalities remain available and intact throughout deployment. In practice, however, cameras can fail due to hardware faults, become occluded by objects or the robots own body, or suffer from signal degradation in uncontrolled settings [7]. Robust robot behaviour under such sensor dropout conditions is therefore not merely desirable property but fundamental requirement for real-world deployment yet it remains largely unaddressed in the imitation learning literature, where even methods explicitly designed for robustness [8] show limited recovery under complete camera failure.

sections.title: Reinforcement Learning with Soft Fusion for Multimodal Imitation Learning under Missing Modalities
sections.content: Although significant progress has been made in multimodal learning with missing modalities [9, 10, 11, 12, 13], these methods are not directly applicable to robotic manipulation for three compounding reasons. First, several are confined to homogeneous, domain-specific modalities: MRI sequences [14, 15] or physiological signals [16], rather than the heterogeneous vision-and-language streams of robot learning. Second, methods that do span broader modality types including omni-modal retrieval [17], visual recognition [12, 13], and general classification or segmentation [9, 10, 11] target static prediction tasks and cannot produce the sequential action outputs required for manipulation. Third, and most critically for deployment, all of these approaches require retraining or fine-tuning of learnable parameters to accommodate new missing-modality pattern, making zero-shot sensor dropout at inference time infeasible. Summary of contributions. RL-guided demonstration retrieval. We introduce the first use of reinforcement learning to select the most relevant expert demonstration from training library for imitation learning. PPO-trained policy operates over BFS-augmented candidate sets and learns to rank neighbours by their usefulness to the current query, replacing heuristic distance-weighted aggregation with learned selection strategy. Soft cross-attention fusion. Rather than committing to single retrieved demonstration, lightweight crossattention fusion head performs soft, attended aggregation over the top-K RL-ranked candidates, producing more reliable action predictions than hard argmax selection particularly under noisy retrieval conditions. Zero-shot missing-modality robustness without retraining. RL4IL requires no policy retraining to handle sensor dropout at inference time. When camera fails or becomes occluded, dedicated per-modality RL retrieval policy identifies donor demonstrations from frozen training library, and soft imputation head reconstructs the missing embedding via cross-attention over the top-ranked donors leaving all other pipeline components unchanged. State-of-the-art performance on LIBERO benchmarks. Experiments across LIBERO-Spatial, LIBEROObject, and LIBERO-Goal demonstrate that RL4IL substantially outperforms all baselines including BC, DisBC, BESO-ACT, and DisDP under complete camera dropout, while requiring no policy network training. The remainder of this paper is organised as follows: Section 2 reviews related work, Section 3 details the RL4IL framework, Section 4 presents experimental results and ablation studies, and Section 5 concludes.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: Imitation learning for manipulation. Behaviour Cloning [3] and its descendants including diffusion policies [4], action-chunking transformers [5], and growing family of Vision-Language-Action models [18, 19, 20, 21, 22, 23, 24, 25, 26] have achieved strong results on structured benchmarks such as LIBERO [2]. However, all of these methods assume every training modality remains available at deployment. Camera position has been identified as the largest single source of generalisation failure in visual IL [27], and demonstration quality is key determinant of policy performance [28, 3]. Robustness to sensor dropout. Masked-modality training for RL policies [7] and multi-view disentanglement [29, 30] improve robustness to camera failure in RL settings. On the IL side, DisDP [8] structures sensor inputs into shared and private representations to reduce reliance on individual cameras, but still requires full policy retraining and collapses under complete camera dropout  (Table 1)  . Retrieval-based policies. Recent retrieval-augmented IL methods condition policies on retrieved demonstrations [4, 5], but all assume complete observations at query time. Multimodal Learning. Missing-modality multimodal learning has attracted considerable attention, but predominantly in domains and settings orthogonal to ours. Domain-specific methods M3AE [14] and ShaSpec [15] for missing MRI sequences, CIMSleepNet [16] for incomplete physiological signals require full retraining and do not generalise beyond their target modalities. More general frameworks such as SimMLM [9] and the in-context retrieval approach in [10] span broader task types but are confined to static classification or segmentation and rely on end-to-end trainable architectures. Prompt-based [12, 11, 13] and omni-modal [17] methods target visual recognition or retrieval and still require updating learnable parameters to handle new missing-modality patterns.

sections.title: 3 Method
sections.content: We consider robot that learns from library of expert demonstrations Dtr = {(xi, yi)}N i=1, where each demonstration xi is observed through sensor modalities such as RGB cameras and natural-language task instructions and

sections.title: Reinforcement Learning with Soft Fusion for Multimodal Imitation Learning under Missing Modalities
sections.content: yi denotes the associated supervision signal, which is recorded action sequence in imitation learning settings or scalar label in regression and classification tasks. At deployment, one or more sensors may fail or become unavailable, producing test observation with missing-modality subset. Rather than retraining the system or relying on handcrafted imputation rules, our method retrieves the most relevant expert demonstration from Dtr and replays its recorded response, adapting gracefully to sensor dropout through learned imputation and retrieval pipeline. For each modality {1, . . . , }, frozen pretrained encoder Em : Xm Rdm maps the raw sensory input to dm-dimensional embedding z(m) . No encoder weights are updated during training; the robots perceptual representations are fixed, and all learning occurs in the retrieval and imputation modules described in this section. = Em x(m) (cid:16) (cid:17) To place all modalities on common footing, we apply modality-specific z-score normalisation to each embedding block and define modality-fair distance that weights each modalitys contribution equally regardless of its embedding dimensionality; full details are given in Appendix A.

sections.title: 3.1 Neighbours Sets
sections.content: For query observation o, the candidate neighbour set C(o) is defined as the training demonstrations closest to under the modality-fair distance d: CkNN(o) = arg topknDtr (cid:2)d(o, n)(cid:3). (1) The neighbourhood size is fixed hyperparameter shared across all experiments. These demonstrations form the seed set from which the BFS-augmented candidate pool is constructed in the following subsection.

sections.title: 3.2 BFS-Based Candidate Set and RL Selection
sections.content: The base prediction aggregates all members of C(o) equally (subject to distance weighting), but not all neighbours are equally informative. We therefore train reinforcement learning (RL) policy that selects single best neighbour from C(o), replacing the weighted aggregate with learned point prediction.

sections.title: 3.2.1 BFS-Augmented Candidate Set
sections.content: To give the RL agent richer and more label-diverse pool to choose from, we augment each training points initial neighbour set through breadth-first search (BFS) on training k-NN graph. Formally, let = (V, E) be the directed graph whose vertex set is = Dtr and whose edge set is = (cid:8)(s, v, wsv) (cid:12) (cid:12) C(s), wsv = d(s, v)(cid:9), (2) where C(s) is the initial neighbour set of (k-NN). For training source with label ys, we run depth-limited shortest-path search (Dijkstra with depth bound D) from outward through G. For every visited node v, the graph distance δ(s, v) is the length of the shortest path from to using at most hops; this distance is updated during the search whenever shorter path is found, consistent with correct Dijkstra behaviour. The BFS candidate set of is then B(s) = (cid:8)v {s} (cid:12) (cid:12) δ(s, v) < , depth(s, v) D(cid:9), (3) together with their graph distances {δ(s, v)}vB(s). We retain B(s) only if it contains at least one node whose label matches ys; otherwise the point is excluded from RL training. The oracle for source is the node in B(s) with the same label and the smallest graph distance: n(s) = arg min δ(s, v). (4) Before presenting B(s) to the policy, its members are randomly shuffled at each training epoch, so the oracle is not always at predictable position and the policy cannot exploit ordering artefacts. vB(s), yv=ys

sections.title: 3.2.3 Reward and Training Objective
sections.content: The immediate reward for selecting action (i.e. candidate va) is defined relative to the oracle: r(s, a) = L(cid:0)yva , ys (cid:1) + L(cid:0)yn(s), ys (cid:1), where is the task-specific loss and n(s) is the oracle defined in (4). For classification tasks we use the 0/1 loss, giving r(s, a) = 1[yva = ys] 1[yn(s) = ys]. Because the oracle always achieves the minimum possible loss within the candidate set, {1, 0}: the reward is 0 when the policy matches the oracle and 1 when it selects wrong-label candidate. The policy is trained with Proximal Policy Optimisation (PPO) using the clipped surrogate objective [31]: JPPO(θ) = (cid:104) min (cid:16) ρt(θ) ˆAt, clip(ρt(θ), 1ε, 1+ε) ˆAt (cid:17)(cid:105) + αent H(πθ), (10) where ρt(θ) = πθ(at st)/πθold (at st) is the probability ratio, ˆAt = r(s, at) is the single-step advantage (no value baseline is used), ε is the clipping threshold, H(πθ) is the entropy of the policy distribution, and αent is the entropy bonus coefficient. Only training points with B(s) 2 are used, since singleton set admits no meaningful choice. After each epoch the policy is evaluated on held-out validation split and the checkpoint with the best validation F1-micro is retained for final test evaluation.

sections.title: 3.2.4 Soft Candidate Fusion via Cross-Attention
sections.content: Hard argmax selection of single neighbour discards potentially useful signal from the remaining high-ranked candidates in B(s). To address this, we augment the pipeline with lightweight fusion head that performs soft, attended aggregation over the top-K candidates ranked by the RL policy. After the policy assigns score rj to every node in the candidate set, the highest-scoring candidates are retained. Let RD denote the query embedding and j=1 RD the selected candidate embeddings with associated labels {yj}K {vj}K j=1. The fusion head projects query and candidates into shared df -dimensional space using separate linear layers, applies H-head scaled dot-product attention to obtain per-candidate weights {αj}, and computes soft attended label = (cid:80) αjyj. two-layer MLP then refines using the attention-weighted context vector = (cid:80) denotes the projected key), producing the final scalar prediction ˆy = MLP([c; y]). The same fusion mechanism extends straightforwardly to classification tasks. In that setting, each candidate label yj {0, . . . , 1} is first mapped to one-hot vector ej {0, 1}C, and the soft attended representation becomes = (cid:80) αjej C1, which can be interpreted as label-distribution estimate over the neighbourhood. The MLP head then takes [c; p] Rdf +C as input and produces logits over classes, trained with cross-entropy loss. At inference, the predicted class is ˆy = arg maxc MLP([c; p])c. The remainder of the architecture query/key projections, multi-head attention, and context vector computation is identical to the regression case, so single implementation supports both tasks by switching only the output layer and loss function. sections.title: 3.2.5 Missing-Modality Imputation via Per-Modality RL.
sections.content: When camera fails or becomes occluded during robot operation, the corresponding modality block is absent from the query embedding q, rendering the concatenated representation incomplete and causing the modality-fair distance to an unreliable basis for retrieval. We address this with two-stage imputation pipeline that precedes label prediction. For each potentially missing modality m, dedicated RL policy π(m) is trained to select the best donor from modality-mrestricted kNN index of the training set only training samples where modality is available are eligible donors. The partial query embedding is formed from the remaining < present modalities using re-normalised modality-fair ϕ

sections.title: Reinforcement Learning with Soft Fusion for Multimodal Imitation Learning under Missing Modalities
sections.content: scaling with in place of . BFS candidate set is built over this donor index, and the imputation oracle is defined as the donor whose modality-m embedding z(m) minimises the squared ℓ2 distance to the ground-truth embedding z(m) . The policy is optimised with PPO using the same ranking reward = (rankoracle rankaction)/(K1) [1, +1], where candidates are ranked by their squared ℓ2 distance to the ground truth. At training time the ground-truth modality-m embedding is available and used for oracle definition and BFS seeding; at inference it is replaced by zero-vector cold start, and the policy selects donors based on the partial-query context alone. Soft Imputation via Attended Donor Aggregation. Hard selection of single donor copies one embedding vector verbatim, which can be noisy if no single donor closely matches the missing modality. To address this, we optionally augment the imputation stage with soft imputation head that aggregates the top-K imp RL-ranked donors via crossK attention, analogous to the prediction fusion head. Let {d(m) j=1 denote the selected donor embeddings. The head projects the partial query and each donor into shared dimp-dimensional space via separate linear layers, applies Himp-head scaled dot-product attention to obtain weights {βj}, and computes soft attended embedding z(m) = (cid:80) . two-layer MLP then refines z(m) using the attention-weighted key context, producing the final imputed block ˆz(m) = MLP([z(m); c(m)]) Rdm . The soft imputation head is trained with supervised MSE objective = ˆz(m) z(m) 2 2 on training samples where modality is present (simulated as missing), with the imputation RL policy frozen throughout. The resulting imputed block replaces the missing entry in the raw embedding, after which the full embedding is reconstructed with =3 modalities and passed to the prediction pipeline. βjd(m) imp } i

sections.title: 3.2.6 Extension to Imitation Learning
sections.content: Although the preceding formulation is stated for supervised prediction tasks, the same pipeline applies directly to imitation learning (IL) settings in which each training sample is an expert demonstration rather than labelled input output pair. In this context, demonstration consists of sequence of multimodal observations such as visual frames from one or more cameras and natural-language task instruction together with the corresponding recorded action sequence. Each demonstration is treated as single multimodal sample xi: frozen pretrained encoders map each observation modality to fixed-dimensional embedding, the embeddings are modality-fair normalised and concatenated to form fi, and the action sequence plays the role of the label yi. At test time, the initial observation of new episode is embedded in the same way, the RL policy with fusion head selects the most relevant training demonstration from the BFS candidate set, and the corresponding action sequence is replayed open-loop in the environment. Task success thus depends entirely on the quality of the retrieved demonstration, requiring no policy network training. When one or more observation modalities are unavailable at test time for example due to camera failure the missing-modality imputation pipeline (Section 3.2) reconstructs the absent embedding block from donor demonstrations before retrieval proceeds, leaving the rest of the pipeline unchanged.

sections.title: 4 Experiments
sections.content: In this section we report results of RL4IL on three robotic manipulation benchmarks from the LIBERO suite [2]: LIBERO-Spatial, LIBERO-Object, and LIBERO-Goal. LIBERO-Spatial consists of ten tasks that require spatial reasoning about object positions on tabletop; LIBERO-Object consists of ten tasks centred on object-centric manipulation under varying object configurations; and LIBERO-Goal consists of ten tasks defined by goal-conditioned instructions that require longer-horizon reasoning. All three suites share the same observation space: an agent-view RGB camera, an in-hand RGB camera, and natural-language task instruction, giving =3 modalities per demonstration. Each suite provides 50 expert demonstrations per task recorded via teleoperation. For all three suites we evaluate under the two conditions: (i) agent-view camera absent (cam 0 masked), and (ii) in-hand camera absent (cam 1 masked). In these conditions, the missing camera embedding is reconstructed by our per-modality imputation pipeline before retrieval proceeds; Performance is measured as task success rate: the fraction of rollouts that complete the task within 260 steps, averaged over 3 random seeds with 25 rollouts per task per seed. We compare against these baselines: Behaviour Cloning (BC), Disentangled BC (DisBC), BESO-ACT, BESO-ACT with modality dropout training (BESO-ACT-Dropout), and the proposed Disentangled Diffusion Policy (DisDP). Results are shown in table 1. Effect of soft fusion. fig. 1 compares the full RL4IL pipeline, which uses soft cross-attention fusion head to aggregate signals from the top-ranked candidate demonstrations, against hard-argmax variant that commits to single retrieved demonstration without any aggregation. Soft fusion consistently matches or outperforms the hard-argmax variant across all three benchmark suites and both camera-dropout conditions, demonstrating that attending over

This is particularly evident under challenging dropout conditions where individual retrieved demonstrations may be imperfect, and aggregation over multiple candidates compensates for retrieval noise. Effect of modality-fair normalisation. fig. 2 compares RL4IL with and without modality-fair z-score normalisation and dimension scaling. Although the unnormalised variant occasionally achieves higher scores on individual conditions, this is an artefact rather than genuine improvement: without normalisation, the modality with the largest embedding norm dominates the distance metric, which can accidentally favour retrieval when that single modality happens to be sufficient for the task at hand. This dominance, however, is dataset-dependent and does not generalise reliably. Normalisation removes this bias by ensuring all modalities contribute equally to the retrieval distance regardless of their dimensionality, producing more consistent performance across all suites and dropout conditions and making the method behaviour more predictable when sensors fail. Effect of training epochs. We investigate the effect of training epochs {1, 5, 10, 15, 20} on task success rate under camera dropout across all three LIBERO suites. As shown in fig. 3, performance on LIBERO-Spatial and LIBEROObject remains relatively stable across epochs. In contrast, LIBERO-Goal exhibits clear upward trend with longer training, demonstrating that additional training epochs yield meaningful gains under goal-conditioned tasks. Notably, RL4IL maintains competitive performance even at epoch 1 across all suites, which surpasses the reported results of all baselines in table 1. Effect of top-K fusion candidates. We investigate the effect of the number of top-K fusion candidates (K = imp {4, 8, 12, 16, 32}) on task success rate under camera dropout, and results shown in fig. 4. Across all three suites, performance is generally stable for moderate values of K, suggesting that RL4IL is not highly sensitive to this hyperparameter within reasonable range.

sections.title: 5 Conclusion
sections.content: We presented RL4IL, reinforcement learning-guided retrieval framework for imitation learning that is robust to sensor dropout without requiring any policy retraining. At its core, PPO-trained policy operates over BFS-augmented candidate sets to select the most relevant expert demonstration from frozen training library the first application of reinforcement learning to nearest-neighbour selection in imitation learning. soft cross-attention fusion head

sections.title: Reinforcement Learning with Soft Fusion for Multimodal Imitation Learning under Missing Modalities
sections.content: With Normalisation Without Normalisation LIBERO-Goal LIBERO-Object LIBERO-Spatial No Cam No Cam1 No Cam0 No Cam1 No Cam0 No Cam1 Figure 2: Ablation study: modality-fair normalisation vs. unnormalised embeddings. LIBERO-Goal LIBERO-Object LIBERO-Spatial No Cam0 No Cam1 R c S 1 0.8 0.6 0.4 0.2 a e S 1 0.8 0.6 0.4 0. 0 1 5 10 15 1 5 10 15 20 5 10 15 20 Epochs Epochs Epochs Figure 3: Effect of training epochs on task success rate under camera dropout. Solid blue = agent-view absent (No Cam0); dashed red = in-hand absent (No Cam1). Shaded regions denote 1 standard deviation. aggregates signals from the top-ranked candidates, consistently outperforming hard argmax selection across all evaluated conditions. When modality is absent at inference time, dedicated per-modality RL retrieval policy identifies donor demonstrations and soft imputation head reconstructs the missing embedding via cross-attention, restoring the full observation representation without any modification to the downstream pipeline. Experiments on three LIBERO benchmark suites LIBERO-Spatial, LIBERO-Object, and LIBERO-Goal demonstrate that RL4IL substantially outperforms all baselines under complete camera dropout, achieving success rates of up to 0.733 where the strongest prior method, DisDP, reaches only 0.295. Ablation studies confirm that soft fusion, modality-fair normalisation, and the RL retrieval policy each contribute meaningfully to overall performance, and that RL4IL maintains competitive results even with single training epoch. These results suggest that retrieval-based imitation learning, augmented with learned ranking and soft imputation, is practical and effective strategy for deploying robotic systems in real-world environments where sensor availability cannot be guaranteed. Future work may explore extending RL4IL to online settings, incorporating richer temporal context into the retrieval query, and scaling the demonstration library to larger and more diverse task distributions. acknowledgments This research work has received funding from the EU through the ARTEMIS project (grant agreement No 101136299) and PRESERVE project (grant agreement No 101168309). Authors confirm that they did experiments on GPU resources at Bournemouth University, and also there is no conflict of interests.

sections.title: Reinforcement Learning with Soft Fusion for Multimodal Imitation Learning under Missing Modalities
sections.content: A Modality-Specific Normalisation and Modality-Fair Distance Raw embeddings produced by heterogeneous encoders can differ dramatically in scale and per-dimension variance, which would cause the distance measure introduced below to be dominated by modalities with larger raw magnitudes. To place all modalities on common footing we fit modality-specific z-score statistics on the training set. For each modality we compute the element-wise mean and standard deviation over all training samples: µm =

Any dimension whose standard deviation falls below ϵ > 0 is clamped to 1, preventing division by zero while leaving near-constant dimensions with negligible influence on distances. Normalised embeddings are then obtained via z(m) = z(m) µm σm , (12) applied element-wise. At inference time, test embeddings are normalised with the training statistics µm, σm, so no test information leaks into the normalisation. Given two normalised multimodal samples zi = {z(m) }M m=1, we define their dissimilarity as m=1 and zj = {z(m) }M d(i, j) = (cid:118) (cid:117) (cid:117) (cid:116)

The inner division by dm converts each modalitys squared ℓ2 error into mean squared error per dimension, so that modality with larger embedding space does not disproportionately dominate the distance. The outer factor 1/M averages the per-modality contributions so that the distance is comparable across pairs regardless of how many modalities are present.

sections.title: B State and Candidate Features
sections.content: The state feature vector ϕ(s) Rd+2 for source is ϕ(s) = (cid:2)fs; Var(yB(s)); B(s)(cid:3), (14) where fs Rd is the normalised concatenated embedding of s, Var(yB(s)) is the variance of the labels of all nodes in the BFS set, and B(s) is the set size. The candidate feature vector ψ(s, v) Rd+4 for B(s) is ψ(s, v) = (cid:20) fv; δ(s, v) maxuB δ(s, u) ; depth(s, v) maxuB depth(s, u) ; rankd(v) B(s) 1 (cid:21) ; yv yB(s) , (15) where depth(s, v) is the number of hops in the shortest path, rankd(v) is the 0-indexed rank of when B(s) is sorted by ascending graph distance, and yB(s) is the mean label in the set. All normalised features lie in [0, 1]. Test-Time Set Size Estimation At test time the true label of query is unknown, so the BFS cannot be stopped at the oracle. Instead we estimate the appropriate set size using the training set sizes of the querys initial neighbours (UNN or k-NN seeds) weighted by their inverse distances: ˆS(o) = max nC(o) B(n) 10 (16)

sections.title: Reinforcement Learning with Soft Fusion for Multimodal Imitation Learning under Missing Modalities
sections.content: where B(n) is the training BFS set size of neighbour n, pre-computed and stored during training. The BFS from is then expanded until exactly ˆS(o) nodes have been collected, after which the policy selects the best candidate. This ensures that the distribution of set sizes seen by the policy at test time matches the distribution encountered during training.

sections.title: D Hyperparameters
sections.content: All hyperparameters are shared across the three LIBERO benchmark suites (LIBERO-Spatial, LIBERO-Object, and LIBERO-Goal) unless stated otherwise. We group them by pipeline stage.

sections.title: Encoders and Embeddings
sections.content: Each of the three modalities agent-view camera, in-hand camera, and natural-language instruction is encoded by frozen CLIP ViT-B/32 model, producing dm = 512-dimensional embedding per modality. For visual modalities, Nframes = 8 frames are sampled uniformly from each demonstration and their CLIP image features are averaged to form the per-demonstration visual embedding. No encoder weights are updated at any stage.

sections.title: Neighbourhood and Graph Construction
sections.content: The initial neighbour set used for BFS seeding is constructed with kapprox = 20 approximate nearest neighbours (used to form the candidate pool) and kseed = 5 seed neighbours passed to the BFS. The k-NN graph over which BFS expands uses kgraph = 5 edges per node. BFS is bounded by maximum depth of = 6 hops and maximum of 200 visited nodes per source, whichever is reached first. The training set is split with validation fraction of 0.15; all BFS statistics are computed on training embeddings only, and test embeddings are normalised using training-set statistics. Prediction RL Policy (PPO) The RL policy is trained with Proximal Policy Optimisation for 30 epochs using the clipped surrogate objective with clipping threshold ε = 0.2. The entropy bonus coefficient is set to αent = 104. The Adam optimiser is used with learning rate 3 104 and gradient norm clipping at 1.0. Rollout minibatches contain 128 samples.

sections.title: Soft Prediction Fusion Head
sections.content: The fusion head attends over the top-K = 32 RL-ranked candidate demonstrations. Query and key projections map embeddings into shared space of dimension df = 128, split across = 4 attention heads. The head is trained for 30 epochs with the Adam optimiser at learning rate 3 104, batch size of 32, and gradient norm clipping at 1.0. Imputation RL Policy (PPO) dedicated per-modality imputation policy is trained independently for each camera modality. Training runs for 50 PPO epochs with minibatches of 64 samples, entropy coefficient αent = 104, learning rate 3 104, and gradient norm clipping at 1.0. The clipping threshold and optimiser are identical to the prediction RL policy. The language modality is always present and is never subject to imputation.

sections.title: Soft Imputation Head
sections.content: The soft imputation head aggregates the top-K imp = 32 RL-ranked donor embeddings via cross-attention. Projections map into shared space of dimension dimp = 64 across Himp = 2 attention heads. The head is trained for 30 epochs under supervised MSE objective with the Adam optimiser at learning rate 3 104, batch size 32, and gradient norm clipping at 1.0. The imputation RL policy is frozen throughout.

sections.title: Rollout Evaluation Protocol
sections.content: Task success is evaluated across 3 random seeds, with 25 rollouts per task per seed, for total of 75 rollouts per task. Each rollout is allowed maximum of 260 environment steps. Success rate is reported as the fraction of rollouts in which the task is completed within the step budget, averaged over seeds, following the evaluation protocol of DisDP [8].
